1: A fulfillment: the eighth day — the day beyond the seven-day creation week, the first day of a new order. Christ rises on the first day of the week (John 20:1), the “eighth day” of the early church's calendar — the day of new creation inaugurating the new priestly order. The seven-day ordination confinement gives way to the eighth-day ministry; the burial-and-waiting gives way to the resurrection morning.
2: A fulfillment: Aaron offers for his own sin first. Hebrews 7:27 draws the explicit contrast: “unlike those high priests, he has no need to offer sacrifices daily, first for his own sins and then for those of the people, since he did this once for all when he offered up himself.” The Aaronic pattern (self-offering first) is the system's built-in acknowledgment of its own inadequacy. Christ bypasses this step — he has nothing to atone for in himself.
3: A type: a male goat for the people's sin offering. The goat as sin offering prefigures Christ as the one who “bore the sin of many” (Isaiah 53:12); John identifies him as the comprehensive sin-bearer: “the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world” (John 1:29). Both Yom Kippur goats (Lev 16) — one killed, one sent away — find their fulfillment in Christ alone.
4: A type: “today YHWH will appear to you” — the promised theophany at the end of the sacrificial sequence. Christ's resurrection is the ultimate theophany-after-sacrifice: the Father's visible validation of the Son's completed offering. The cult's goal — divine presence — is achieved through sacrifice; Christ achieves it once for all (John 17:24).
5: A type: the congregation assembles to witness the first sacrificial service. The crowd at Golgotha (Luke 23:35: “the people stood by, watching”) witnessed the definitive sacrifice. The assembly at Pentecost (Acts 2:1-6) witnesses its validation — the theophanic fire of the Spirit at Pentecost is the NT counterpart to the divine fire of Leviticus 9:24.
6: A type: obedience to the prescription leads to the appearing of divine glory. Christ's perfect obedience to the Father's will (Luke 22:42: “not my will, but yours, be done”) was the path by which the Spirit was poured out and the glory of God made visible in the resurrection. Obedience and theophany are structurally linked in the Levitical system — and in Christ.
7: A type: “draw near to the altar and offer” — the command to approach. Christ needed no command from a mediator; he came willingly: “I lay it down of my own accord. I have authority to lay it down, and I have authority to take it up again” (John 10:18). The command-structure of the Levitical system (Moses commanding Aaron) is replaced by the Son's own initiative in perfect alignment with the Father's will.
8: A type: Aaron slaughters his sin offering with his own hand. Christ is simultaneously the priest who offers and the offering that is presented: “through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God” (Hebrews 9:14). The structural separation between priest and victim in Leviticus is collapsed in Christ, who is both the one who offers and the one offered.
9: A type: Aaron's sons bring the blood to him. Christ presents his own blood in the heavenly sanctuary with no intermediary: “he entered once for all into the holy places, not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption” (Hebrews 9:12). The slaughter-site-to-altar transport is overcome in Christ who is both sacrifice and presenting priest.
10: A type: fat portions burned for Aaron's personal sin offering. Christ had no personal sin offering — the critical point Hebrews makes (Hebrews 7:26-27). The Aaronic system's requirement that the high priest offer for himself before offering for the people is a structural limitation built in to signal the system's own inadequacy and to point toward the priest who needs no such provision.
11: A fulfillment: Aaron's sin offering burned outside the camp. Hebrews 13:12: “so Jesus also suffered outside the gate in order to sanctify the people through his own blood.” The burning outside the camp — removing the sin-bearing sacrifice completely from the community's midst — is enacted in Christ's crucifixion outside Jerusalem's walls.
12: A type: Aaron's burnt offering — total self-dedication in the priestly role. Christ's complete dedication to the Father's mission: “he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross” (Philippians 2:8). The burnt offering has no human portion — it ascends entirely to YHWH; Christ's self-offering was wholly for the Father's glory and the people's salvation.
13: A type: Aaron's sons present the pieces of the burnt offering. The disciples' apostolic witness is the NT counterpart: “you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the end of the earth” (Acts 1:8). The church does not re-sacrifice; it presents what was once sacrificed by bearing witness to the completed offering.
14: A type: the entrails and legs are washed before burning — every part pure. Christ's sacrifice was wholly pure: “like that of a lamb without blemish or spot” (1 Peter 1:19). There was no element of his offering that required cleansing. In bearing sin, he underwent the ultimate washing — the removal and destruction of sin through the cross, whose purity and sin-bearing are both absolute.
15: A fulfillment: Aaron presents the sin offering for the people — the priesthood's primary function. Christ's definitive enactment: “when Christ had offered for all time a single sacrifice for sins, he sat down at the right hand of God” (Hebrews 10:12). The Aaronic high priest presents the people's sin offering annually; Christ presents the once-for-all offering that requires no repetition.
16: A fulfillment: Aaron offers “according to the rule” (kamishpat). Christ fulfills every prescribed rule: “I have not come to abolish the Law or the Prophets but to fulfill them” (Matthew 5:17). His priestly ministry is not an innovation but the perfect execution of every rule the system prescribed — executed at the level of reality rather than shadow (Hebrews 10:1).
17: A type: the grain offering added to the burnt offering. Christ is both the bread of life (John 6:35) and the sacrificed Lamb (John 1:29). His Eucharistic words at the Last Supper bring these together: “this is my body” (grain offering) and “this is my blood of the covenant” (sacrificial blood). The combined offering of Leviticus 9:17 is the type of the Eucharistic self-gift.
18: A type: the peace offerings for the people — the offering of reconciliation. Christ is our peace: “since we have been justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ” (Romans 5:1; Ephesians 2:14: “he himself is our peace”). The peace offering's communion meal is fulfilled in the Lord's Supper: a participation in the reconciliation Christ enacted (1 Corinthians 10:16).
19: A type: the sons present the fat portions designated for YHWH. The church's role is not to sacrifice but to present: “present your bodies as a living sacrifice” (Romans 12:1). The community's participation in presenting the portions is now enacted in Spirit-led self-dedication in response to Christ's completed work.
20: A type: the fat on the breast — the divine portion covers the priestly portion. Christ's sacrifice encompasses both what he offers to the Father (the fat burned on the altar) and what he gives to his people (the breast eaten by the priests). His death is simultaneously the offering to the Father and the nourishment of the church.
21: A fulfillment: Aaron waves the breast and right thigh before YHWH — the wave offering enacts presentation-and-return. Christ's resurrection is the great wave offering: presented to the Father in death, received, and returned as the living Lord. “Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep” (1 Corinthians 15:20) — the firstfruits waved before YHWH are the type; Christ's resurrection is the reality.
22: A fulfillment: Aaron lifts his hands toward the people and blesses them. Luke 24:50-51: “lifting up his hands he blessed them. While he blessed them, he parted from them and was carried up into heaven.” Christ's ascension is the ultimate Aaronic blessing — the High Priest performs the Numbers 6:24-26 benediction as he departs. Every spiritual blessing in the heavenly places is now poured out in him (Ephesians 1:3).
23: A fulfillment: Moses and Aaron enter the tent together; the glory of YHWH appears. The Pentecost event is the NT counterpart: the Spirit descends on the assembled disciples (Acts 2:1-4). The theophanic fire that validates Aaron's first sacrifice is the type; the tongues of fire at Pentecost are the fulfillment — the Father's confirmation that Christ's sacrifice has been accepted.
24: A fulfillment: divine fire consumes the sacrifice; the people shout and fall on their faces. The resurrection and Pentecost are the NT enactments: divine fire (the Spirit) validates the sacrifice (the cross); the crowd's response is both conviction and rejoicing. Acts 2:37: “cut to the heart” — the falling-on-their-faces response to the fire that validates the sacrifice. The fire of Leviticus 9:24 descends at Pentecost as the Spirit who empowers the church.
